tracks.title: Classification Lab
tracks.description: Compare classification methods for binary and multi-class problems

use_case: Interpretable binary classification with probability output
Fast probabilistic classification, especially with text or sparse data
data_assumption: Linear decision boundary in log-odds space
Feature independence (rarely true but often works)
interpretability: High — coefficients directly interpretable as odds multipliers
Medium — class probabilities visible, but independence assumption is opaque
tuning_burden: Low — regularization strength (C) is the main lever
Very low — minimal hyperparameters, fast to train
overfitting_risk: Low — strong bias assumption keeps variance down

use_case: Robust general-purpose prediction; built-in feature importance
State-of-the-art accuracy on tabular data; wins Kaggle competitions
data_assumption: No distributional assumption; handles mixed types well
No distributional assumption; benefits from feature engineering
interpretability: Medium — feature importance available; individual trees inspectable
Low-Medium — SHAP values needed for full explanation
tuning_burden: Low-Medium — ntrees, max_depth, mtry
High — learning rate, n_estimators, max_depth, subsampling, regularization
overfitting_risk: Low — bagging + random features decorrelate trees
Medium-High — early stopping critical; overfits noisy data easily

use_case: Non-parametric classification when decision boundary is complex
High-dimensional classification; effective with kernel trick for nonlinear boundaries
data_assumption: Distance meaningful in feature space; features must be scaled
Finds maximum-margin hyperplane; works in transformed feature space via kernel
interpretability: High conceptually (vote of neighbors) but no global structure
Low — black box in kernel space; margins not easily explained
tuning_burden: Low — k and distance metric only; but expensive at inference
Medium — C (regularization) and kernel parameters (gamma for RBF)
overfitting_risk: High with small k; curse of dimensionality at high-d
best_assignment: MBAN 5560 Assignment 4 — SVMs and Neural Networks

use_case: When you need interpretability and a probability estimate
When you need accuracy and can tolerate a black-box model
data_assumption: Linear log-odds relationship between features and outcome
No linearity assumption; captures interactions automatically
interpretability: Very high — each coefficient has a direct odds-ratio interpretation
Medium — feature importance available but individual prediction reasoning is complex
tuning_burden: Low — few hyperparameters
Medium — ntrees, depth, mtry
overfitting_risk: Low — OOB error is a free honest estimate

tracks.title: Optimization Lab
tracks.description: Compare optimization and decision analysis methods for business problems

use_case: Continuous allocation problems: resource scheduling, mix optimization
Discrete decisions: facility location, project selection, yes/no choices
data_assumption: All decision variables are continuous and non-negative
Some or all variables must be integers (often binary 0/1)
interpretability: High — shadow prices give economic value of relaxing constraints
Medium — solution clear but combinatorial complexity grows fast
tuning_burden: Low — standard simplex or interior point; exact solution
High — branch-and-bound; NP-hard in general; solver limits apply
common_metric: Objective function value, shadow prices, slack/surplus
Objective function value, LP relaxation gap, MIP gap
best_assignment: MBAN 5540 Assignment 2 — LP Formulation and Duality

use_case: Multi-stage sequential decisions where state evolves over time
Single-stage or simultaneous allocation of continuous resources
data_assumption: Markovian structure: future depends only on current state
Linearity in objective and constraints
interpretability: Medium — Bellman equation logic clear; state space can explode
High — primal solution + dual shadow prices fully transparent
tuning_burden: Medium — must define state, stages, and recursion correctly
Low — standard formulation maps directly to solver
common_metric: Value function, optimal policy by state
best_assignment: MBAN 5540 Assignment 2 — LP Formulation and Duality

use_case: Choose among discrete alternatives under uncertainty using expected value
Test how optimal solution changes as parameters vary — before committing
data_assumption: Probabilities assigned to uncertain outcomes; outcomes quantified
Base LP/IP solution available; ranges of validity computable
interpretability: High — decision trees are visual and intuitive
High — ranging tables directly show decision stability
tuning_burden: Low — enumerate outcomes, assign probabilities, compute EMV
Low — output directly from LP solver sensitivity report
overfitting_risk: N/A — but probability estimates may be poorly calibrated
common_metric: Ranging: RHS and objective coefficient ranges
best_assignment: MBAN 5540 Assignment 2 — LP Formulation and Duality

tracks.title: Database Design Lab
tracks.description: Compare database design concepts for relational schema and query development

use_case: Capture business entities and relationships before building the database
Eliminate redundancy and anomalies in an existing or candidate schema
data_assumption: Business requirements can be modeled as entities with attributes and relationships
Functional dependencies between attributes are identifiable
interpretability: Very high — visual; non-technical stakeholders can validate
Medium — dependency rules require technical understanding
tuning_burden: Low — iterative with stakeholder feedback
Medium — requires identifying all FDs before decomposing
overfitting_risk: N/A — design process, not model fitting
common_metric: Completeness: all entities, attributes, relationships captured

use_case: Enforce data integrity: no orphaned records, no duplicate entities
Translate ERD + normal forms into a deployable relational table structure
data_assumption: Primary keys uniquely identify each row; FK references valid rows
ERD and normalization work complete; ready for implementation
interpretability: High — PKs and FKs are explicit and checkable
High — table structure is directly readable
tuning_burden: Low — specify PK, FK, UNIQUE, NOT NULL constraints at creation
Medium — requires balancing normalization against query performance
common_metric: Referential integrity, no anomalies on insert/update/delete
Schema handles all use cases; JOINs work correctly

use_case: Extract insights and KPIs from an existing database using SELECT/JOIN/GROUP BY
Design the table structure that makes those queries efficient and correct
data_assumption: Tables exist with appropriate keys; data is clean and normalized
Business requirements translated into entity-relationship structure
interpretability: High — SQL is declarative and readable
High — schema diagrams visual and stakeholder-readable
tuning_burden: Medium — complex JOINs and window functions require practice
Medium — requires normalization and ERD work first
